title: Towards a microscopic model for an electronic quantum charge liquid
authors: Jacob R. Taylor, Sankar Das Sarma, Seth Musser
abstract: We provide a route to constructing an electronic quantum charge liquid (QCL), a state made up of fermions at fractional filling of a lattice that does not break translation. Starting with spinless fermions at filling $ν=3/2$ we pair them to get bosons at filling $ν=3/4$ per unit cell. The tetramer model, a generalization of the dimer model, on the square lattice is evaluated as a candidate bosonic QCL at filling $ν= 3/4$. It is shown that these models exhibit a local $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry. Upon numerical study of a family of tetramer wavefunctions it is found that while one is gapless due to $\mathrm{U}(1)^3$ symmetry at least one other can be definitively shown to be gapped. The gapped nature of this state, along with its $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry, leads us to propose that it is an example of the elusive bosonic QCL displaying the minimal $\mathbb{Z}_4$ topological order. We conclude by discussing possible extensions to other lattice geometries, electronic QCLs, and to Rydberg atoms.
summary: This paper proposes a microscopic model for an electronic quantum charge liquid using a tetramer model on a lattice. By mapping fermions to bosons, the authors investigate a state that preserves translation symmetry but possesses topological order. Using tensor network simulations, they identify a gapped phase with Z4 topological order and a gapless phase driven by emergent symmetries. The work also extends these findings to triangular lattices and suggests potential realizations in Rydberg atom systems.
main_problem: The paper aims to construct a microscopic model for an electronic quantum charge liquid (QCL), a translation-symmetric phase intermediate between a Wigner-Mott insulator and a metal.
main_result: The authors identify a gapped state in a tetramer model that realizes minimal Z4 topological order and demonstrate that certain configurations are gapless due to emergent U(1) symmetries.
method: The study uses tensor network methods, specifically representing wavefunctions as Projected Entangled Pair States (PEPS) and analyzing them via a transfer matrix approach on cylinders.
model_or_system: The model consists of spinless fermions at filling nu=3/2 on a square or triangular lattice, which is mapped to a bosonic tetramer model (a generalization of the dimer model) at filling nu=3/4.
key_observables: Correlation length, spectral gap, Z4 topological order, and local Z4 flux conservation.
important_parameters: The parameter theta (controlling tetramer configuration weights), cylinder circumference L, and bond dimension chi.
main_assumptions: Numerical results are limited by finite system sizes (L) and finite bond dimensions (chi); the gap in the equal mixture state may be too small to resolve.
figures_summary: Fig 1 explains QCLs and tetramer models; Fig 2 shows the RVB wavefunction and transfer matrix; Fig 3 details the transfer matrix tensor; Fig 4 compares correlation length scaling for straight vs. bent configurations; Fig 5/6 illustrate the transfer matrix construction and scaling on a triangular lattice.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QCL concept, defines the fermionic-to-bosonic mapping and the tetramer model, presents numerical results for square lattice configurations (gapless vs. gapped), extends the analysis to triangular lattices, and discusses implications for Rydberg atoms.
why_interesting: The discovery of a microscopic model for a new type of topological order (Z4) and the connection to Rydberg atom constraints provides a potential platform for studying exotic many-body phases in programmable quantum simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. The wavefunction and transfer matrix. (a) The RVB wavefunction for the tetramer model on a 4 × 4 lat- tice. Straight tetramers are colored blue, partially bent tetramers are colored green, and fully bent tetramers are col- ored red. Each tetramer configuration is weighted by a factor of cn = cotn θ, where n is the total number of bends in the configuration (with blue tetramers having none, green having one, and red having two bends). (b) The transfer matrix, T , for a semi-infinite cylinder with a circumference of four sites. The transfer matrix is built out of the rank-4 tensor T applied on each site. The exact form of T is given in Fig. 3. The dot- ted line indicates the periodic...

FIG. 1. A visual explanation of minimal quantum charge liquids. (a) A system of charge-1 bosons at ν = 1/2 fill- ing per unit cell that does not break translation requires a half-charged background anyon per unit cell [1]. This is illus- trated with the half-circle background anyons, which square to the circular elementary boson. (b) For a system of charge-1 spinless fermions at ν = 1/2 a charge-2 boson can be made by Cooper pairing. To get a half-charged background anyon then requires taking a quarter of the Cooper pair. Four back- ground anyons are required to return to the bosonic vacuum, leading to the Z4 toric code in this case. (c) An illustration of why the dimer model can be viewed...

FIG. 3. An explicit form of the tensor Tijαβ displayed in Fig. 2(b) and discussed in the main text. When i, j, α, β are one of the configurations displayed above then Tijαβ takes the value at left for that row. For all other i, j, α, β, Tijαβ = 0. To find the flux (mod 4) emanating from a vertex subtract the value on an incoming arrow from the value on an outgo- ing arrow and take the result (mod 4). All vertices have an outgoing flux of 1 (mod 4).

FIG. 4. The upper bound on correlation length, ξ, vs. cylinder circumference, L, for fully straight and fully bent tetramer configurations, illustrated in the insets. (a) The upper bound on correlation length for fully straight configurations. As L →∞ we see that ξ is diverging. Increasing the bond dimension from χ = 300 to χ = 600 enhances the divergence. These are indicative of gaplessness, coming from a U(1)3 symmetry. The symmetry can be seen by partitioning the lattice into four sublattices, indicated by the different colors in the inset, and is discussed in the main text. (b) The upper bound on correlation length for fully bent configurations. Now as L →∞the correlation length ξ...

FIG. 5. The transfer matrix and our numerical process for the triangular lattice. (a) The transfer matrix is built out of rank-6 tensors T. We show only a section of this. Because of the geometry of the triangular lattice different layers of the transfer matrix must be shifted by half a lattice site relative to one another. (b) For ease of computation the rank-6 tensor T can be decomposed into two rank-4 tensors contracted to- gether, TA and TB. Thus by doubling the number of tensors we can return our transfer matrix to the square lattice form. In addition to this decomposition we must then add a shift to ensure TA is coupled to TB in the next layer.

FIG. 6. The correlation length, ξ, vs. cylinder circumference, L, for the equal weight tetramer superposition on the trian- gular lattice. For L = 8, 12 the bond dimensions χ = 200 and 300 are found to agree, while there is a large disagreement in correlation length for L = 16. Going to larger bond dimen- sion χ = 400 at L = 16 appears to be closer to the ξ value obtained at χ = 300, and appears consistent with a saturating correlation length. Unfortunately it was not feasible to run further analysis for L = 20, where attempting χ = 400 took several months before timing out.
